> "candidates need to learn the concept of Representation - best done with reference to theories of representation where there is a variety of student-friendly materials available - such as **Hall, Van Zoonen, hooks, Gilroy, Alvarado**."

This concerns the representation of class, age, gender and ethnicity in Section A analysis.

## Status in the course

Gilroy is not currently taught at BNDS. The S1 representation block ([[classes/media-studies/unit-plans/9607-s1-unit-4-representation|U4]]) uses Hall, van Zoonen, Mulvey and hooks.

entities/roland-barthes.md.body:
# Roland Barthes

Roland Barthes is the most-used theorist in this course, and one of two named theorists for the AS year (the other is [[entities/stuart-hall]]).

## His two contributions

**Semiotics — signs, codes and meaning.** This is the theory hook for Component 1 research on both briefs (film and magazine): Barthes' ideas about signs, codes and meaning.

**Narrative codes and signification.** In Component 2 textual analysis, you can use some of Barthes' narrative codes to add explanatory depth, along with the idea of signification: what is **denoted** at a descriptive level, and what is suggested, implied or coded at a **connotative** level. This is an option for explanatory depth, not required content.

## Where he fits in the course

- **[[concepts/media-language]]** — denotation and connotation is the ladder the whole of Unit 2 climbs. The denotation-before-connotation discipline is built into CS1 in [[classes/media-studies/lesson-plans/9607-s1-lesson-01-the-four-key-concepts|L01]] and marked in A1.
- **[[concepts/narrative]]** — the enigma code gives narrative analysis explanatory depth without heavy theory, and connects directly to the C1 film-opening research question "how does the film establish an enigma from the outset?"
- **C1 research** — signs and codes are the vocabulary you use to annotate real film openings and magazine covers.

## In class

Barthes is taught across [[classes/media-studies/lesson-plans/9607-s1-lesson-02-semiotics-how-images-mean|L02]] and [[classes/media-studies/lesson-plans/9607-s1-lesson-03-sign-types-and-codes|L03]] as the semiotics spine of Unit 2.

entities/marshall-mcluhan.md.body:
# Marshall McLuhan

Named, with [[entities/neil-postman]], as an origin of [[concepts/media-ecology]].

## What Cambridge attributes to him

> "The notion of media ecology can be traced back to **Neil Postman** and **Marshall McLuhan**, but its contemporary relevance is clearly driven by the saturation of all aspects of contemporary existence with media and media technologies." (SoW p.25)

That is the complete attribution. The Cambridge documents credit no specific idea, book, or coinage to him — no "the medium is the message," no global village, no hot and cool media.

## Media ecology in the course

Media ecology is a compulsory 30-mark synoptic paper — half of Component 4. The syllabus defines it as "the complex environments within which media texts, producers, distributors, technologies and audiences exist" (Syllabus 2027 p.19).

entities/neil-postman.md.body:
# Neil Postman

Named, with [[entities/marshall-mcluhan]], as the origin of [[concepts/media-ecology]].

## What Cambridge attributes to him

> "The notion of media ecology can be traced back to **Neil Postman** and **Marshall McLuhan**, but its contemporary relevance is clearly driven by the saturation of all aspects of contemporary existence with media and media technologies." (SoW p.25)

This is the complete attribution. No specific idea, work, or argument is credited to him — no *Amusing Ourselves to Death*, no technopoly, no distinction between the typographic and televisual mind.

## The method for media ecology

For media ecology, Cambridge gives a **method** rather than a theory (SoW pp.25–26):

- historical contrast — "What did the media landscape look like before 1990?" against your own media diary
- a premise — contemporary life is saturated with media and "mediatised"
- a temporal orientation — "contrasting the present with the past, and analysing the present in order to develop ideas about the future"

The before/after contrast does most of the analytical work, and the exam rewards "informed, imaginative analysis" (SoW p.26) grounded in contemporary evidence. Separately, the syllabus requires you to "use key theories of media studies" (Syllabus 2027 p.13).

The current form of this topic uses a three-era periodization and four structured focus areas — see [[concepts/media-ecology]].

Grade 12, Semester 4 content. See [[classes/media-studies/unit-plans/9607-course-map|the course map]].

entities/stuart-hall.md.body:
# Stuart Hall

Stuart Hall's ideas come up in this course in two ways: **encoding and decoding**, and **representation**.

## Encoding and decoding

This is an extension activity: exploring Stuart Hall's ideas of encoding and decoding when making sense of a media text. Extension activities are differentiated content for stronger learners, not core AS content.

The standard three reading positions — **dominant, negotiated, oppositional** — are widely taught. Treat them as course-standard knowledge.

Hall's model is a way of thinking about how audiences interpret the media, including how they may interpret the same text in different ways, using a variety of theories or ideologies.

## Representation

Hall is also studied as a **representation theorist**. When you learn the concept of Representation, you can do this with reference to theories of representation, including **Hall, Van Zoonen, hooks, Gilroy, Alvarado**.

## Where it leads

Encoding/decoding is the bridge from thinking about audiences as a target to thinking about audiences as interpreters. It feeds forward into [[concepts/power-and-the-media]], where the question of whose reading counts becomes the topic itself.

entities/slavoj-zizek.md.body:
# Slavoj Žižek

The only theorist Cambridge names in the [[concepts/power-and-the-media]] debate.

You are asked to consider his idea of the media's "Standard Operating Procedure," and also to test whether that diagnosis still holds: "In the years since the publication of this article, it is now also worth considering how much has changed in terms of increased representation of different ethnicities in media productions - and also analyse how and why degrees of change have occurred." Test the diagnosis rather than citing it as settled.

## What Cambridge attributes to him

> "Media ownership will be one factor to consider, and there are examples of media outlets being directly influenced, or self-censoring, because of their owners, but more significant will be what **Slavoj Žižek** calls the media's **'Standard Operating Procedure'** – its reproduction of ideological 'truths' about which voices count for more than others, its reduction of complex conflicts to spectacle, and the general reinforcing of 'common sense'." (SoW p.25)

Three mechanisms, all usable as analytical categories:

1. reproduction of ideological "truths" about whose voice counts
2. reduction of complex conflicts to spectacle
3. general reinforcement of "common sense"

## Why Cambridge reaches for him here

The sentence structure is the argument: ownership and self-censorship are the obvious answers to "where does media power live," and Cambridge explicitly says something else is "more significant." The point is that power operates **without anyone issuing an instruction** — through the ordinary, unremarkable procedures by which stories get told.

In an essay, this is the move from a conspiratorial reading (owners dictate content) to a structural one (procedures reproduce common sense). The second is far more defensible and much harder to argue against.

## Connection to spectacle

"Reduction of complex conflicts to spectacle" links directly to [[entities/guy-debord]] in the [[concepts/postmodern-media]] topic — the same concept, used in two of the three debates. Cambridge does not flag the overlap: learn spectacle once and you can use it in both.

shared/bnds-ai-policy.md.body:
# BNDS AI Policy

This is a school-wide policy, from the **A-Level Student Guide 2025-26 §VI** (pp.22–24), within the Academic Honesty Policy. It applies to every course.

Rather than a blanket permit-or-ban, it defines **six graded levels**. Each assignment declares which level applies.

## The six levels (Guide pp.23–24)

| Level | Name | What is allowed |
|---|---|---|
| **0** | Human-Only Work | You produce everything without AI help. No disclosure required |
| **1** | AI for Ideation | AI only to clarify the task, surface key terms, or map existing knowledge — **not to generate fresh concepts**. You still do your own brainstorming. Typical: definitions, locating foundational references, checking feasibility, keyword lists for literature searches |
| **2** | AI for Structuring | Level 1 + AI organizes material — outlines, templates, flowcharts. **No full paragraphs, code blocks, calculations or images generated** |
| **3** | AI for Refinement | Levels 1–2 + AI edits or polishes your draft: grammar, style, citation format, code linting. **Keep a before-and-after or tracked-changes file** showing what you accepted |
| **4** | AI Co-Creation | Levels 1–3 + you and AI share the labor. Substantial parts AI-generated or transformed, then shaped by you. **Track who did what and cite the model and version** |
| **5** | AI-Generated for Review | Levels 1–4 + the whole piece is AI-produced, submitted only so humans can critique or annotate it. **Label it "AI-generated"**; grading focuses on your analysis, not the AI text |

> [!important] Always check the AI Level for each assignment
> Some assignments **ban AI entirely** — the handbook names exams and personal reflections — and others actively encourage it.

## Attribution requirements (Guide p.22)

Whenever you use AI, you must:

1. **Cite the tool** — e.g. "Generated with assistance from [AI Tool Name]"
2. **Explain how it was used** — e.g. "Used for brainstorming but all analysis is my own"
3. **Cite the prompt** you used

Your instructor may additionally require a disclaimer or a reflection on AI use.

## Permitted and prohibited (Guide p.22)

**Permitted:** assisting learning (brainstorming, clarifying concepts, summarizing readings, generating practice questions); enhancing productivity (grammar checks, citation formatting, organizing research); encouraging creativity (AI output as a *starting point* for original work, with attribution); developing digital literacy.

**Prohibited:** submitting AI-generated work as your own without citation or permission; using AI to replace independent analysis, original thought or personal reflection unless explicitly allowed; spreading unverified AI content — you must verify AI-generated content for accuracy.

Misuse follows the Academic Honesty Policy: resubmission, grade penalties, or disciplinary action (Guide p.23).

## The Art & Design worked example (Guide p.24)

- **Level 1** — AI summarizes Bauhaus colour theory so you can plan palette experiments
- **Level 2** — AI offers a clean portfolio layout grid (page order, caption placeholders, margins) that you fill with original work
- **Level 3** — AI polishes the grammar of an artist statement and reorganizes paragraphs for flow
- **Level 4** — Midjourney, Stable Diffusion or similar generates an image that you then collage, annotate and contextualize, **detailing the AI's role in the process**

## ⚠ Where school policy meets exam-board policy

These are separate regimes, and the exam board is the stricter authority on assessed work. Both must be satisfied.

| | Position |
|---|---|
| **BNDS** | Six levels; Level 4 co-creation explicitly permitted, including generated images in Art & Design, with attribution |
| **Cambridge 9479 (2028-30)** | AI-generated images permitted **only** with your own role demonstrated and justified — see [[classes/a-level-art-design/a-level-art-design]] |
| **Cambridge 9607 (2027)** | *"Cambridge International has a policy on the use of generative AI by candidates in coursework. The inappropriate use of AI should be treated as a form of plagiarism"* (Syllabus 2027 p.30). The 2027 spec does not otherwise address AI; the 2028-30 spec permits it with documented prompts and justification |

**The practical rule for coursework:** BNDS Level 4 may be permissible for classroom tasks, but anything going to Cambridge as assessed coursework is governed by the syllabus for the exam year. Where they differ, the syllabus wins — and the handbook's attribution requirements (cite the tool, explain the use, cite the prompt) are exactly what the 2028-30 Art & Design spec asks for, so satisfying BNDS properly also satisfies Cambridge.

This is the **2025-26** edition of the handbook.

shared/bnds-assessment-framework.md.body:
# BNDS Assessment Framework

The school-wide grading scheme from the **BNDS A-Level Student Guide 2025-26**, applied by every course. This page is the single reference for the rules below. The framework, curving, notice, retake and AI rules are current; syllabus specifics change per subject and per exam year.

## Grade composition

| Category | Weight | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Attainment** | **50%** | Major, summative. Syllabus content only, exam conditions, curved to CIE past-paper data; should produce a reliable Forecast Grade. Project completions and portfolio milestones count. |
| **Course Skills** | **20%** | Minor, formative. Skills/content relevant to the syllabus, flexible format: quizzes, drafts, practice tasks, homework. |
| **Learning Behaviour** | **10%** | Glossary upkeep, blog punctuality, screening/feedback participation, case-study contributions, correcting work, submitting on time. |
| **End of Term Exam** | **20%** | Summative, previously covered content, sat in the school exam window (S1 2026-27: Mon 2027-01-11 – Mon 2027-01-18). |

## Notice for assessments

**Attainments carry at least one week's notice** (AL Student Guide p.13). Non-attainment assessments carry **72 hours**.

## Standing rules

- **4–6 attainments per semester**; none in the first two teaching weeks.
- **Attainment dropping** — one per semester, late in the semester, but only if the class has **≥6 attainment grades in MB**, and you have missed none, have no plagiarism or cheating record, no unexcused absences, are always on time, and attend all required grade/programme meetings (Guide p.12). ⚠ **All four courses run four attainments, so no student is eligible to drop one.** This is a result of how the courses are designed, not of your conduct.
- **One grade per week** posted on MB — at least one grade per subject each week (Guide p.12). Learning Behaviour postings fill the weeks with no CS task or attainment.
- **Marking returned within 5 working days** (Guide p.12).
- **Homework capped at 30 minutes per subject per day** (Guide p.13); break homework ≈ 3–4 hours per ten-day holiday.
- **Extensions** must be requested **at least 24 hours before the deadline** (Guide p.13).
- **Absence from an assessment** requires contacting the subject teacher **24 hours before the due time**; illness needs a doctor's note. ⚠ *"If no communication is made with the subject teacher in advance you may be given 0, even if this is due to illness"* (Guide p.8).
- **Late-work cutoff:** three weeks before the last school day of the semester (Guide p.13). For S1 this falls inside the winter break, so the operative date will be confirmed — see [[calendar|school academic calendar]].
- **Assessment content:** only what has been taught and practised — *"You should have seen similar content or formats beforehand"* — though "the context in which the knowledge or skills are applied may be novel" (Guide p.9).
- Formative (CS) tasks placed next to an attainment are **direct practice for it**.
- **Seniors get a reduced assessment load** in the week before the 1 Nov and 1 Dec university deadlines (Guide p.13).

## Curving and grade boundaries

Attainments are **not recorded as raw marks** (Guide pp.11–12):

- Curved against **CIE past-paper data**, "allowing for where students are on their learning trajectory."
- **More generous in G10, stricter in G11** and later in the year.
- Grades "reflect your learning trajectory, not what you would score on a CIE exam today."
- Past-paper grade thresholds are "the best starting point" for setting boundaries.
- **Moderation** applies where an attainment runs across multiple classes taught by different teachers; the Head of Department checks large between-class differences and may adjust curves or boundaries.
- *"You will always be told how grade boundaries and curves are decided."*

An attainment "should make a reliable **Forecast Grade**" (Guide p.11).

## Grade conversion (Guide p.11)

| CIE scale | A-Level grade | BNDS GPA |
|---|---|---|
| 90–100 | A* | 4.3 |
| 80–89 | A | 4.0 |
| 70–79 | B | 3.5 |
| 60–69 | C | 3.0 |
| 50–59 | D | 2.5 |
| 40–49 | E | 2.0 |
| below 40 | U | 0 |

**Failing is below 50%** (Guide p.15). **At or below 70%** triggers the Academic Concern Steps — your advisor and the A-Level Director are informed and support measures follow (Guide p.13).

## Retake policy (Guide §IV, pp.15–16)

You get one opportunity to retake a failed course, in the holiday after the semester, with 2–3 teacher meetings for support. The work is "just as challenging" and similar in quantity to the original. The grading policy is explained before finals and **cannot be changed once agreed**. A signed academic-honesty declaration is required; plagiarism means immediate failure with no resubmission.

**A passed retake earns the equivalent of a D**, not the grade actually achieved. A failed retake leaves the original grade standing. Under force majeure, more than 50% completion plus written parent-signed evidence can pass; without evidence, fail.

Graduating seniors get only **two weeks** for a retake, because their Summer Holiday starts early.

classes/a-level-art-design/9479-departme.body:
# 9479 Department Handbook

Your Art & Design handbook covers the visual elements, the principles of design, the three components, the assessment objectives with checklists, the four disciplines, theme selection, artist studies, the Personal Study, and portfolio samples.

## Some numbers in it are from an older specification

The handbook was written for the earlier 9479 specification, not the one this cohort sits. **Do not rely on the sheet counts, hours, word counts or mark totals printed in it.** Use the current syllabus for those. The teaching content, prose and structure are still fine — this is only a numbers problem.

| Feature | Handbook says (old spec) | Use instead | Page |
|---|---|---|---|
| C1 name | "Component 1: Coursework" | Component 1: Portfolio (*coursework* is still a correct informal name) | p.34 |
| C1 mark | single mark out of 100 | mark grids | p.34 |
| C2 submission | three sheets of A2, photographed and mounted | all-digital submission | p.35 |
| C1 submission | five sheets of A2 | — | p.34 |
| C3 submission | eight sheets of A2 | — | p.37 |
| C3 written analysis | 1000–1500 words | **2000–2500 words** ← use this | pp.36, 104 |
| C3 mark | out of 100, "weighted to 200" | — | p.37 |
| AOs | AO1 Record · AO2 Explore · AO3 Develop · AO4 Present, flat weighting | new per-component splits | p.39 |

The handbook gives the C2 timed test as 15 hours (p.35); the correct duration will be confirmed in class.

The [[classes/a-level-art-design/a-level-art-design|course page]] also warns against taking numbers from the older support documents.

## Things in the handbook that disagree with themselves

1. **Personal Study word count.** pp.36 and 104 say 1000–1500 words; pp.114–115 say 2000–3000 words and "should not exceed 3000 words." Ignore both — use **2000–2500 words** from the syllabus.
2. **Tint.** p.18 is correct: *tone* is adding darker shades, *tint* is adding white. The p.19 sub-glossary is wrong where it says "tint — Another word for shade."
3. **AO names.** Three different sets appear (pp.39, 72, 84). Treat p.39 as the correct one: Record / Explore / Develop / Present.

## What is in it

### Visual Elements (pp.7–23)

Six elements, each a definition plus a full-bleed plate: **Line** (Fred Sandback), **Shape** (Escher, *Sky and Water II*), **Space** (Doug Wheeler), **Tone** (Wolfgang Mattheuer), **Color** (pp.18–21, with a CMYK note and six example plates), **Texture**.

Form is not covered in this chapter. The fullest element vocabulary — including Form, with adjective banks for all six — is at **p.81** in the Artist Studies chapter, e.g. Texture: "flat, raised, smooth, rough, coarse, shiny, glossy, reflective, scratched, matt, satin, frosted, visible, smooth, blended, impasto, washes, glazes, stippled, splattered, bold, timid, lively, layered."

### Principles of Design (pp.24–30)

- **p.25 "How to Read Images"** — the four-stage method (Description · Formal Analysis · Interpretation · Judgement) over a grid of 15 paired opposites for visual communication: balanced/unstable · gestural/technical · sharp/diffuse · symmetry/asymmetry · active/still · distorted/realistic · solitary/juxtaposed · flatness/depth · transparent/opaque · regular/irregular · subtle/amplified · vivid/neutral · unified/fragmented · ordered/loose · light/dark
- **p.26 "17 Key Principles of Design"** — Alignment · Hierarchy · Contrast · Repetition · Proximity · Balance · Color · Space · Emphasis · Proportion · Rhythm · Pattern · Movement · Variety · Unity · Lines · Shapes
- **pp.27–30** — worked demonstrations of eight: Emphasis (focal point), Hierarchy, Scale/Proportion (size · ratio · divisions), Contrast (in shape · color · scale · layout), Repetition, Unity & Variety, Balance (symmetrical · asymmetrical)

### The four disciplines (pp.48–57)

**Fine Art** (painting, drawing, sculpture, photography, printmaking, mixed media, assemblage) · **Graphic Communication** (illustration, printmaking, packaging, branding, advertising, signage, typography) · **Three-Dimensional Design** (sculpture, ceramics, product, architecture, interior, environmental, set design, jewellery) · **Textiles and Fashion** (fashion design/illustration, costume, constructed textiles, screen printing, batik, digital-printed textiles, surface pattern).

You may focus on one discipline or combine several. Two distinctions:

- **Fine Art responds to a theme only**; the other three respond to a theme **or a brief** (pp.50–57).
- **3-D Design uniquely requires** you to consider "the setting in which the final outcome will be situated, e.g. interior/exterior, urban/rural" (p.55).

### Themes (pp.66–72)

Guidance on *choosing* a theme (this is Component 1-facing, p.66). Nine qualities of an appropriate theme (personal relevance · breadth and depth · creativity and originality · flexibility · researchability · visual and conceptual interest · educational value · relevance to the assessment objectives · engagement with broader issues), a seven-step selection guide, then the assignment: complete a theme selection form for at least four possible themes with related artists and possible artworks, choose one, and justify it. Submitted by the end of the week.

### Assessment Objectives (pp.38–47)

Take the assessment objectives and all rubrics from the current syllabus, not from this handbook. The handbook's AO **checklists (pp.41–47)** are still useful as scaffolding — for example, AO1's runs seven questions from "collected images to show your inspiration and stimuli?" through "clearly linked all of your work to your starting point?" — as long as the objectives they hang off come from the syllabus.

### Personal Study (pp.104–118)

Three study types (p.105), which can be combined:
- **Technical** — media, surfaces, tools, techniques
- **Stylistic** — signs, symbols, metaphor, meaning, figurative and formal elements
- **Contextual** — art and society; social, historical and cultural contexts; theories of art

The integration rule (p.105): *"the study will form the starting point of your practical coursework. The two must link together and inform each other i.e. they are not separate elements but dependent on each other. Your success depends on this point."*

Use the 2000–2500 word count from the syllabus, not the figures printed in this chapter.

### Portfolio samples (pp.119–205)

Around 87 pages of sample work, captioned ("Final Outcome", "SAMPLE 1 / Component 1").

## AI use

The examples in [[shared/bnds-ai-policy|the school AI policy]] are Art & Design examples, and its attribution requirements align with what the current syllabus asks for on AI-generated images.

classes/pre-a-level-art-design/lesson-pl.body:
# PAL S1 Lesson 08: The Theme Shoot and the Contact-Sheet Edit

[[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/pre-a-level-art-design|Pre A Level Art & Design]] · [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/unit-plans/pal-s1-unit-2-photography|Unit 2]] · U2·L3 · ← [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/lesson-plans/pal-s1-lesson-07-light-shadow-and-the-series-theme|L07]] | [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/lesson-plans/pal-s1-lesson-09-a2-the-photographic-series|L09]] →

## At a glance

| | |
|---|---|
| Session | W9, one Wednesday double (~90 min) |
| Dates | Wed 2026-10-28 |
| Focus | The main theme shoot; then the contact-sheet edit — "shoot many, keep few, know why" |
| Deliverable | ≥20 new theme frames; a contact sheet with 12 selects and 6–8 series candidates marked |
| Feeds | **CS2 is collected this session** (composition set + contact-sheet edit); everything feeds A2 next week |
| Homework | Finish edits on the 6–8 candidates; draft a sequence order; MB posting |

## Goal

This lesson works two skills back to back: **abundance** (a focused shoot with a hard quota) and then **selection** (the contact-sheet ritual — see everything small and together, keep few, write why). Editing enters here too, in its honest order: crop first, then tone, then color — and never on the only copy.

## How it runs

- **Warm-up (5 min)** — theme pulse-check: state your theme and frame count so far. If you are stuck or without a theme, re-theme now from your L07 shortlist.
- **The theme shoot (35 min)** — on campus, solo, on your theme: **minimum 20 new frames.** The discipline rule: every frame states **one composition decision and one light decision** (thirds or broken? high or low? side-light or back-light?). Be ready to answer: "what are you deciding here?"
- **Teach: the edit (15 min)** — back in the room. The contact sheet: why editors look at everything small, together, before choosing. Then the edit order — the same in **Photoshop, Affinity, or any editing platform**, phone editors included: **crop and straighten first → exposure and contrast → color last → work non-destructively** (adjustment layers in Photoshop/Affinity; on a phone, duplicate then edit — the original always survives). File hygiene in one line: your pictures are files; know where they live.
- **The contact-sheet edit (25 min)** — assemble all your theme frames (last week's homework + today's shoot) into a contact sheet — a Bridge/Lightroom contact sheet, the gallery grid screenshotted into the sketchbook, or printed strips. **Circle 12 keepers, then mark 6–8 series candidates.** Give each cut a reason; give each keep a reason. This, plus your L06 circuit frames, is **CS2 — collected before the close.**
- **Close (10 min)** — homework: finish the edits on your candidates (crop → tone → color, originals kept) and draft a sequence order for next week's A2 session.

## Assessment

**CS2 — composition practice set + contact-sheet edit** — is collected today. Marked for **framing and selection**: honest reasons on keeps *and* cuts count more than pretty frames. Register: [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/assessments/pal-s1-assessments|PAL S1 Assessments]].

# PAL S1 Lesson 06: Framing and Viewpoint

[[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/pre-a-level-art-design|Pre A Level Art & Design]] · [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/unit-plans/pal-s1-unit-2-photography|Unit 2]] · U2·L1 · ← [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/lesson-plans/pal-s1-lesson-05-a1-the-final-observational-drawing|L05]] | [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/lesson-plans/pal-s1-lesson-07-light-shadow-and-the-series-theme|L07]] →

## At a glance

| | |
|---|---|
| Session | W7, one Wednesday double (~90 min) — Unit 2 opens, first session after National Day |
| Dates | Wed 2026-10-14 |
| Focus | Framing, viewpoint, rule of thirds — composing with a camera instead of a pencil |
| Deliverable | Best-3 selection from HW1 with written reasons; a five-viewpoint circuit shoot with three selects |
| Feeds | **HW1 due today**; the circuit frames become part of CS2's composition set (W9) |
| Homework | Ten frames placed deliberately on the thirds + five deliberately breaking them, one line on which felt better; MB posting |

## Goal

The unit's motto: **"shoot many, keep few, know why."** You arrive with twenty photographs of one subject. This session shows you that the interesting decisions happen *before* the shutter — where you stood, what you put in the frame, what you left out — and that choosing three from twenty is a skill with reasons.

## How it runs

- **Warm-up (10 min)** — cameras out — **any camera counts: phone, compact, DSLR** — and the framing grid goes **on** (grid overlay on phones, viewfinder grid on cameras; your standing habit for this unit). Put the vocabulary list into your sketchbook — terms 1–13 (elements, principles, composition); a two-minute match game: term to photo on screen.
- **HW1 harvest (20 min)** — open your twenty photos. Choose your **best three and your worst one**, then explain both choices to a partner with the frames: "I kept this because…" / "This one fails because…". Selection is the unit's core skill.
- **Teach (15 min)** — framing and viewpoint, from the *Seeing Photographs* exemplars: **rule of thirds**, balance (symmetrical vs asymmetrical), horizon placement and tilt, **vantage point**, figure–ground. Each concept is one image and one question ("what did the photographer decide here?").
- **Viewpoint circuit (30 min)** — one subject per pair, five stations: **eye level · high · low · close · far-with-context**. Minimum fifteen frames each. The subject never moves; only you do — viewpoint alone changes the picture's meaning.
- **Close (15 min)** — select three from the circuit, post to MB with a one-line reason each; add thumbnail sketches or printed strips of the selects with notes to your sketchbook. Homework stated.

## Assessment

**HW1** (≥20 photos, one subject, varied viewpoint/light — set 09-30) is due and checked today; marked for looking, not luck. Today's circuit frames and the thirds homework feed **CS2** (W9). Register: [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/assessments/pal-s1-assessments|PAL S1 Assessments]].

# PAL S1 Lesson 07: Light, Shadow and the Series Theme

[[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/pre-a-level-art-design|Pre A Level Art & Design]] · [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/unit-plans/pal-s1-unit-2-photography|Unit 2]] · U2·L2 · ← [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/lesson-plans/pal-s1-lesson-06-framing-and-viewpoint|L06]] | [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/lesson-plans/pal-s1-lesson-08-the-theme-shoot-and-the-contact-sheet-edit|L08]] →

## At a glance

| | |
|---|---|
| Session | W8, one Wednesday double (~90 min) |
| Dates | Wed 2026-10-21 |
| Focus | Light and shadow; what makes a series; you leave with an A2 theme |
| Deliverable | A four-position light circuit; a written, approved series theme in your sketchbook |
| Feeds | A2 (sits W10, Wed 2026-11-04); SB1 sketchbook spot-check runs today |
| Homework | Begin the theme shoot — ≥15 frames on your chosen theme before W9; MB posting |

## Goal

Two halves with one join. First: light has *direction and quality*, and moving yourself (or the lamp) is a decision as expressive as framing. Second: A2 is not five nice photos — it is a **series**, one idea looked at repeatedly with intention. The join is the theme: by the end of the double you have one, in writing, approved.

## How it runs

- **Warm-up (10 min)** — homework pairs: one thirds frame and one broken-thirds frame each, your partner guesses which was deliberate. Add vocabulary terms 20–24 (directional / ambient / soft light…) to your sketchbook glossary.
- **Teach: light (15 min)** — *Seeing Photographs* §1 on screen: **front, side, diffused, back** light, one canonical exemplar each; hard vs soft shadows. Then a live demo: one object, one **continuous lamp**, walked around the four positions — what you see is what you get. Exposure control on whatever you shoot with: phones — **tap to focus, AE/AF lock, exposure slider**; cameras — **AE lock and exposure compensation**.
- **Light circuit (20 min)** — in pairs: one subject (a classmate or an object), four frames — window side-light, backlight, diffused, hard lamp. Same subject, four moods; annotate which position made which mood.
- **Teach: the series (15 min)** — what makes photographs *a series*: one subject, idea, or rule; variation with intention; sequence. Exemplars from the shared library's Photographers collection — **Ueda's project folders, Hara's series, Atget's Versailles, Johansson's towns** — plus Cartier-Bresson's decisive moment from §4. Then the **A2 brief**: 6–8 photographs on one theme, selected from many, sequenced, titled, with a short reflection — made over the next two weeks, submitted in class W10.
- **Theme workshop (15 min)** — shortlist three candidate themes (things you can shoot at school or home this fortnight: doors, hands, red, lunch, shadows, pairs…); talk each through with a partner; **write your chosen one in your sketchbook and get it initialed** before leaving.
- **Close (10 min)** — **A2 announced** and posted on MB; homework: ≥15 theme frames before next Wednesday.

## Assessment

**A2** (photographic series + reflection, Wed 2026-11-04) is announced today — 14 days, two classes ahead. Marking focus: **framing, viewpoint, light, series thinking**. **SB1**, the first sketchbook spot-check, is taken during the studio blocks — it checks progress and upkeep (2–3 pages a week since W1), not a full grading. Register: [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/assessments/pal-s1-assessments|PAL S1 Assessments]].

# PAL S1 Lesson 09: A2 — The Photographic Series

[[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/pre-a-level-art-design|Pre A Level Art & Design]] · [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/unit-plans/pal-s1-unit-2-photography|Unit 2]] · U2·L4 · ← [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/lesson-plans/pal-s1-lesson-08-the-theme-shoot-and-the-contact-sheet-edit|L08]] | next unit: [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/unit-plans/pal-s1-unit-3-collage|U3 Collage]] →

## At a glance

| | |
|---|---|
| Session | W10, one Wednesday double (~90 min) — Unit 2 closes |
| Dates | Wed 2026-11-04 |
| Focus | **A2 sat**: select, sequence, title, and submit your series with its reflection |
| Deliverable | Your final 6–8-image series + written reflection, on MB by close; process trail in your sketchbook |
| Feeds | A2 is the semester's second attainment; your contact sheet and rejects become sketchbook process evidence for SB2/A5 |
| Homework | None new — weekly MB sketchbook posting continues; U3 Collage opens next week |

## Goal

You make A2 in this room, from work you have already done — the double is selection, sequence, and words, not new shooting. Test your series this way: take any one image away — does the group lose something? Add your prettiest off-theme frame — does the group get worse? If you can answer both, you understand what you made.

## How it runs

- **Set-up (5 min)** — criteria on the board: **framing · viewpoint · light · series thinking.** Have all your frames, contact sheet, and edits open. From here it is your own pictures and your own reasons.
- **Selection studio (30 min)** — go from candidates to your final **6–8**. Make two passes: the **kill-your-darlings pass** (your strongest image that doesn't serve the theme goes into the sketchbook, with a note, not the series) and the **weakest-link pass** (if an image needed defending for two sentences, replace or cut it).
- **Sequencing (20 min)** — order is meaning: choose an **opener** (invites), a **development** (varies), a **closer** (lands). Lay out two different orders side by side — gallery view or prints on the table — and keep the one that reads better aloud. Title the series; a title is a claim, not a label ("*Doors of Building B*" < "*Nobody Uses the Front Door*").
- **Reflection (20 min)** — one page, full sentences. Sentence frames: "My series is about … " / "I chose these … from … frames because … " / "The strongest image is … because … " / "My viewpoint/light decision that worked best was … " / "If I shot the theme again I would … ".
- **Submit and close (15 min)** — post your series + title + reflection to MB; put the contact sheet and your annotated rejects into the sketchbook as process evidence. Next week the pictures get scissors — bring nothing, the collage unit supplies its own material.

## Assessment

**A2 — photographic series + reflection**, sat and submitted in class. Marking focus: **framing, viewpoint, light, series thinking** — series thinking is judged on the sequence and the reflection's reasons, not only the individual frames. Returned with feedback within five working days. Register: [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/assessments/pal-s1-assessments|PAL S1 Assessments]].

# PAL S1 Lesson 13: Final Collage Production

[[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/pre-a-level-art-design|Pre A Level Art & Design]] · [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/unit-plans/pal-s1-unit-3-collage|Unit 3]] · U3·L4 · ← [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/lesson-plans/pal-s1-lesson-12-theme-and-composition-drafts|L12]] | [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/lesson-plans/pal-s1-lesson-14-a3-the-final-collage|L14]] →

## At a glance

| | |
|---|---|
| Session | W14, one Wednesday double (~90 min) |
| Date | Wed 2026-12-02 |
| Focus | The final collage built — the assembly session |
| Deliverable | The final collage substantially built (background and middle layers glued, top layer dry-fit); build photographed in stages |
| Assessment today | **SB2 sketchbook spot-check**; the collage finishes under A3 conditions next week |
| Homework | A written finishing list (what remains, in order); missing material gathered; MB posting |

## Goal

This is an assembly session with a plan. Your circled draft becomes the built thing, layer by layer, in the L11 order — background first, the speaker last, glue only behind certainty. By the close, your collage should be **decided everywhere and finished except the top** — next week's A3 double is for the final layer, the edges, and the words, not for starting over.

## How it runs

- **Set-up (5 min)** — drafts and dry-fit photographs out; materials stations open. Build order: **background → large shapes → middle layers → the speaker → text last.**
- **Checkpoint (10 min)** — before you glue: sixty seconds per student at tables — restate your main idea, show your draft, name your first three moves. If your draft no longer convinces you, re-compose now; gluing a doubt wastes a double.
- **Studio: the build (60 min)** — production, with three standing rules: **dry-fit each layer before gluing it**, and photograph every stage (the build sequence is sketchbook process evidence); **stop at the speaker** — the top element and any text stay unglued today so A3 opens with your strongest move still live; **edges are decisions** (torn vs cut, carried from L11). If you are doing a digital remix, work in Photoshop/Affinity — the same order applies and layers are literal. Save the working file; never flatten.
- **Close (15 min)** — write your finishing list: what remains, in what order, needing what. Park work flat; bag and name your scraps (next week's spares). Next week runs under A3 conditions — your hands, your board, your reasons.

## Assessment

**SB2** — the second sketchbook spot-check — runs table by table during the studio block: progress and upkeep since SB1 (10-21), two-minute looks, marks entered the same day. The build itself is assessed next week as **A3**. Register: [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/assessments/pal-s1-assessments|PAL S1 Assessments]].

# PAL S1 Lesson 10: Juxtaposition and Scale

[[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/pre-a-level-art-design|Pre A Level Art & Design]] · [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/unit-plans/pal-s1-unit-3-collage|Unit 3]] · U3·L1 · ← [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/lesson-plans/pal-s1-lesson-09-a2-the-photographic-series|L09]] | [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/lesson-plans/pal-s1-lesson-11-layering-text-and-image|L11]] →

## At a glance

| | |
|---|---|
| Session | W11, one Wednesday double (~90 min) — Unit 3 opens |
| Dates | Wed 2026-11-11 |
| Focus | What collage is; juxtaposition and scale — "two images say a third thing" |
| Deliverable | Vocabulary in the sketchbook; two fast cut-and-paste studies (one juxtaposition, one scale) |
| Feeds | The experiment set that CS3 checks (W13) starts today |
| Homework | Collect raw material — old magazines, printed photos (including your own U2 frames), packaging, tickets; ≥20 usable images by next week; MB posting |

## Goal

In Unit 2 you chose images; now you cut them apart and make them argue. The unit's spine: **combine · meaning** — a collage is not decoration, it is a sentence built from stolen words. Today establishes the two cheapest tools for meaning: **juxtaposition** (two things that don't belong together, together) and **scale** (the wrong size, on purpose).

## How it runs

- **Warm-up (10 min)** — the word itself: *coller*, "to glue." Write four terms in your sketchbook — **collage** (glued combination of found/made images), **papier collé** (glued paper shapes), **photomontage** (collage made from photographs), **assemblage** (collage gone 3D).
- **The lecture (20 min)** — a brisk arc: Picasso and Braque glue newspaper into paintings (1912) → **Hannah Höch** cuts up Weimar Germany (Dada photomontage) → Matisse cuts pure color → Moholy-Nagy and **Rodchenko** build photomontage posters → contemporary digital collage. One question per stop: *what did gluing let them say that painting couldn't?*
- **The two tools (10 min)** — juxtaposition: the *Picasso-and-Höch* diptych side by side — same era, opposite temperaments. Scale: a teacup taller than a building means something; a person smaller than a shoe means something else. The rule: **a collage decision is a meaning decision.**
- **Studio: first cuts (35 min)** — two fast A5 studies from the shared material bin, using scissors only today: (1) a **juxtaposition study** — two images that have no business together, one new meaning; (2) a **scale study** — one element deliberately the wrong size. Glue only after a dry-fit. Title each with one line: "this says …"
- **Close (15 min)** — table walk-through: a partner names the *third thing* your two images say. If they can't, the juxtaposition is decoration, not meaning yet. Studies go into the sketchbook, then the homework brief (the material harvest).

## Materials and safety

Scissors only this session (knives enter at L11 with the safety brief): scissors, glue sticks, A5/A4 cartridge, the class material bin (pre-stocked magazines + printed images). Start collecting your own material today — a collage is only as good as its harvest.

## Assessment

Nothing graded today; both studies open the **experiment set** that CS3 checks at W13. Register: [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/assessments/pal-s1-assessments|PAL S1 Assessments]].

# PAL S1 Lesson 12: Theme and Composition Drafts

[[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/pre-a-level-art-design|Pre A Level Art & Design]] · [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/unit-plans/pal-s1-unit-3-collage|Unit 3]] · U3·L3 · ← [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/lesson-plans/pal-s1-lesson-11-layering-text-and-image|L11]] | [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/lesson-plans/pal-s1-lesson-13-final-collage-production|L13]] →

## At a glance

| | |
|---|---|
| Session | W13, one Wednesday double (~90 min) |
| Dates | Wed 2026-11-25 |
| Focus | Meaning; a main idea chosen; two composition drafts for the final collage |
| Deliverable | A written main idea + two dry-fit drafts, one circled with a reason |
| Feeds | **CS3 collected this session** (the experiment set) (sits W15, 12-09) |
| Homework | Gather any missing material your chosen draft needs; MB posting |

## Goal

This session moves from experiments to a finished work. The question: *what is your collage about?* Not what is in it — what it **says**. Every technique from L10–11 serves one **main idea**, written in one sentence before you start composing. You then make two different drafts of the same idea, because the first arrangement is rarely the strongest.

## How it runs

- **Teach: meaning (15 min)** — **Romare Bearden**: *The Block*, *Three Folk Musicians* — collage as memory and neighborhood, cut paper standing in for a whole world. The pre-photography ancestors make the same point: a Dutch **vanitas** heaps books, globes and jewels together to say *none of this lasts* — objects juxtaposed into a sentence, 1662. Composition is how the sentence gets its grammar.
- **Main idea (10 min)** — write one sentence in your sketchbook: "My collage is about … " — concrete beats abstract ("my grandmother's kitchen" beats "memory"). An accessible fallback is the **silhouette self-portrait**: your profile filled with what fills your head. State your idea aloud at your table; the teacher initials each.
- **Studio: two drafts (45 min)** — make two dry-fit compositions of the same idea on separate boards, deliberately different (one crowded / one sparse, one symmetrical / one tilted). No glue today. Photograph both. Then circle one and write your reason.
- **CS3 + close (20 min)** — **CS3 collected**: the experiment set (juxtaposition, scale, layering, text-and-image + home experiments), checked for range and honest annotation. The final collage is made in class W14–W15 from your circled draft, marked on **juxtaposition, layering, composition, meaning**; the format will be confirmed in class. Post on MB.

## Assessment

**CS3** (experiment set — combination and meaning) is collected today. **A3** (final collage + reflection, Wed 2026-12-09) is announced today — 14 days, two classes ahead. Register: [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/assessments/pal-s1-assessments|PAL S1 Assessments]].

# PAL S1 Lesson 11: Layering, Text and Image

[[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/pre-a-level-art-design|Pre A Level Art & Design]] · [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/unit-plans/pal-s1-unit-3-collage|Unit 3]] · U3·L2 · ← [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/lesson-plans/pal-s1-lesson-10-juxtaposition-and-scale|L10]] | [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/lesson-plans/pal-s1-lesson-12-theme-and-composition-drafts|L12]] →

## At a glance

| | |
|---|---|
| Session | W12, one Wednesday double (~90 min) |
| Dates | Wed 2026-11-18 |
| Focus | Layering (order, edges, transparency) and text as image — the unit's making session |
| Deliverable | A layering study and a text-and-image study added to the experiment set |
| Feeds | **CS3 collects the experiment set next week** (W13, 11-25) |
| Homework | One more experiment at home in any technique; keep harvesting material; MB posting |

## Goal

Two craft skills that separate a collage from a scrapbook page. **Layering**: what is in front is what wins — order, overlap, and edges (torn vs cut) are all decisions. **Text as image**: a word in a collage is not a caption, it is a shape with a voice — cut letters, headlines, and instructions carry tone before they carry information. Every exemplar today uses both at once.

## How it runs

- **Warm-up (5 min)** — last week's studies on tables; thirty-second recall: what was your third thing?
- **Teach: layering (15 min)** — three decisions demonstrated live with the class watching the dry-fit build up: **order** (background first, the "speaker" last), **edges** (torn edge = soft/organic, knife edge = hard/mechanical — tear one, cut one, compare), **transparency** (tissue, tracing paper, thin magazine stock let layers talk). Exemplars: **Barbara Kruger** (text barking over found photographs) and **John Stezaker** (two images spliced so cleanly they become one wrong thing).
- **Blade safety + materials (10 min)** — craft knives enter here: cut away from the hand, blade shallow, cutting mat always, cap when walking, one knife per table returned to the tin. Materials tour: adhesives (glue stick for paper, PVA thin-spread for card), surfaces (cartridge vs card), the tissue/tracing drawer.
- **Studio: two studies (40 min)** — added to the experiment set: (1) a **layering study** — minimum three layers, at least one torn edge and one knife edge, one semi-transparent layer; (2) a **text-and-image study** — one found image + cut-out text that *changes what the image means* (not describes it). Dry-fit, photograph the dry-fit, then glue.
- **Close (10 min)** — pairs swap text studies and read them aloud in the voice the text suggests — if the reading voice is obvious, the tone worked. Reminder: **CS3 collects the whole experiment set next Wednesday**; the digital-remix option is introduced in one line (see below).

## The digital option

You may **remix an analog study digitally** as an extra experiment — layers, masking, drawn interventions — using Photoshop, Affinity, or any editor. Whether the *final* A3 can be digital will be confirmed in class.

## Assessment

Nothing graded today; the two studies (plus L10's two and the home experiment) complete the **experiment set for CS3** (Wed 2026-11-25). Register: [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/assessments/pal-s1-assessments|PAL S1 Assessments]].

# PAL S1 Lesson 14: A3 — The Final Collage

[[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/pre-a-level-art-design|Pre A Level Art & Design]] · [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/unit-plans/pal-s1-unit-3-collage|Unit 3]] · U3·L5 · ← [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/lesson-plans/pal-s1-lesson-13-final-collage-production|L13]] | next unit: [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/unit-plans/pal-s1-unit-4-graphic-design-and-the-poster|U4 Graphic Design & the Poster]] →

## At a glance

| | |
|---|---|
| Session | W15, one Wednesday double (~90 min) — Unit 3 closes |
| Date | Wed 2026-12-09 |
| Focus | **A3 sat**: the final collage finished under assessment conditions, plus the written reflection |
| Deliverable | The final collage + reflection, submitted at close; process trail (drafts, dry-fit photos, scraps) in the sketchbook |
| Feeds | A3 is the semester's third attainment; U4 opens next week |
| Homework | None new — weekly MB posting continues; U4 Graphic Design / Printmaking opens Wed 12-16 |

## Goal

The final layer goes down and the collage starts speaking for itself. Ask the test from L10 one last time before you submit: cover any one element — does the collage lose part of its sentence? If a piece is only decoration, take it off. A3 is a made-in-class attainment: the mark rewards decisions you can point to, in the work and in the reflection.

## How it runs

- **Set-up (5 min)** — work out, finishing lists out, criteria on the board: **juxtaposition · layering · composition · meaning.** From here: your board, your scraps, your reasons.
- **Studio (55 min)** — the finishing list, in order: place and glue the speaker; set text last; resolve edges (every torn edge on purpose, every cut edge clean); sign the back with title and date. If you are working digitally, export your final and print or submit the file with the layered working version.
- **Reflection (15 min)** — one page, full sentences. Frames you can use: "My collage is about … " / "The juxtaposition of … and … says … " / "My layering decision that works best is … because … " / "I placed … at … so that … " / "If I made it again I would …."
- **Submit and close (15 min)** — submit collage + reflection; put the process trail into your sketchbook: both drafts, the dry-fit photographs, and the best rejected scraps, annotated. Next Wednesday the course turns to communicating to an audience: graphic design and the poster, or the linocut route. The A4 route will be confirmed in class then.

## Assessment

**A3 — final collage + reflection**, sat and submitted in class. Marking focus: **juxtaposition, layering, composition, meaning** — meaning is judged on the work *and* the reasons in your reflection. Returned with feedback within five working days. Register: [[classes/pre-a-level-art-design/assessments/pal-s1-assessments|PAL S1 Assessments]].
